title: The Matrix Multiplication View
concept: Conceptually, we compute a layer's outputs by combining all inputs with all weights simultaneously using matrix math, rather than calculating each neuron individually.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Are weights updated during forward propagation?

options.text: No, weights are frozen during the forward pass.
Yes, weights adjust after every layer.
Yes, but only in the input layer.

exercises.explain: Correct! Weights are completely frozen during forward propagation.

cards.title: Calculators with 1,000 hands
cards.body: If you had to multiply 100 pairs of numbers, doing them one by one would take minutes. But what if you had a calculator with 100 hands that could multiply all 100 pairs at the exact same millisecond? That parallel capability is why neural networks rely on matrix multiplication.

cards.title: The Layer Transformation
cards.body: Instead of calculating each neuron's weighted sum one-by-one, a neural network groups all the inputs into one list (vector) and all the weights into a grid (matrix). A single **matrix multiplication** step computes the outputs for all neurons in the layer simultaneously.

cards.title: The master kitchen prep
cards.body: If a restaurant needs to chop 10 onions, 10 carrots, and 10 peppers, one chef doing them one-by-one is slow. Matrix multiplication is like a specialized food processor: you dump all the vegetables (inputs) and blade settings (weights) in at once, press a button, and get the fully chopped mix instantly.

cards.title: Visualizing the grid
cards.body: In a layer with 3 inputs and 2 hidden neurons, there are 6 connections (weights). We arrange the weights in a 3-by-2 grid. Multiplying the 3 inputs by this 3-by-2 grid yields the 2 hidden neuron outputs in one step, making calculation extremely efficient on hardware like GPUs.

cards.title: Matrix math isn't a new algorithm
cards.body: You might think using matrices changes the behavior of the model. In reality, matrix multiplication is just a mathematical shortcut. It produces the exact same numeric results as calculating each neuron individually, but it runs hundreds of times faster on modern chips.

cards.title: Individual vs. Matrix Calculation
cards.body: How we calculate a layer's output:

left.text:
Calculate Neuron 1 output.
Then calculate Neuron 2 output.
Slow, sequential calculation.

right.text:
Inputs combined as a vector.
Weights combined as a grid (matrix).
All outputs calculated in one parallel step.

exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of using matrix multiplication to calculate layer outputs?

options.text: It computes the outputs for all neurons in a layer in a single, parallel step, making it much faster.
It automatically corrects prediction errors without needing backpropagation.
It reduces the number of weights in the network.
It forces all activation values to be positive.

exercises.explain: Correct! Matrix multiplication calculates all outputs in a layer simultaneously, maximizing hardware efficiency.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Calculating a layer's outputs using matrix multiplication produces different numerical results than calculating each neuron individually.
exercises.explain: False! Matrix math is simply a speed shortcut; it yields the exact same numerical results.

exercises.sentence: In neural networks, we arrange a layer's weights into a grid called a ___.
exercises.explain: A matrix is a two-dimensional grid of numbers (weights) used in calculations.

exercises.prompt: Which hardware component is specifically optimized to run these massive parallel matrix multiplications?

options.text: A Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), which has thousands of small cores built for parallel math.

exercises.explain: Correct! GPUs are built specifically to handle parallel matrix math, which is why they are essential for deep learning.

exercises.prompt: A neural network layer has 1,000 inputs and 500 hidden neurons, resulting in 500,000 weight connections. How does the system compute this layer's output during forward propagation?

options.text: It packages the 1,000 inputs as a vector and the 500,000 weights as a 1,000-by-500 matrix, then computes the output in one parallel step.
It calculates each of the 500,000 connections one-by-one sequentially.
It discards 90% of the weights to make calculation possible.
It asks the user to input the pre-calculated weights.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By using a 1,000-by-500 matrix, we run the 500,000 multiplications in parallel, which modern GPUs can do in fractions of a millisecond. Next, let's walk through how a single prediction actually traces through these layers step-by-step!
